source_paper_title: A Single-Loop Algorithm for Decentralized Bilevel Optimization
context:
Page 4, Introduction (end of discussion of heterogeneity assumptions, immediately before 'Main contributions')

Here we emphasize that in this paper we focus on deterministic DBO, because it is already very challenging. Extending the algorithms in this paper to stochastic DBO is definitely another very important task, and we leave it to a future work.
title: Stochastic extension of SLDBO for decentralized bilevel optimization without heterogeneity assumptions
background_latex:
- There are $n$ agents connected by a fixed, undirected communication network represented by a symmetric, doubly stochastic weight matrix $W=(w_{ij})\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$.
- The decentralized bilevel optimization (DBO) problem is
\[\min_{x\in\mathbb{R}^p}\ \Phi(x):=F(x,y^*(x)):=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n F_i(x,y^*(x))\quad\text{s.t.}\quad y^*(x)\in\arg\min_{y\in\mathbb{R}^q} f(x,y):=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n f_i(x,y).\]
- In the deterministic setting, each agent $i$ can evaluate exact derivatives of $F_i$ and $f_i$.
- In a stochastic setting, for each agent $i$ there is a random variable (data sample) $\xi_i$ such that the local objectives are expectations
\[F_i(x,y)=\mathbb{E}_{\xi_i}\big[\widehat F_i(x,y;\xi_i)\big],\qquad f_i(x,y)=\mathbb{E}_{\xi_i}\big[\widehat f_i(x,y;\xi_i)\big],\]
and agents can only access (mini-batch) stochastic gradients/Jacobians/Hessians derived from $\widehat F_i$ and $\widehat f_i$.
- The lower-level objective $f(x,\cdot)$ is (assumed) strongly convex in $y$ so that $y^*(x)$ is well-defined and unique.
- A heterogeneity assumption refers to any condition requiring the local derivatives (e.g. $\nabla \widehat f_i$ or $\nabla^2_{22}\widehat f_i$) to be uniformly close to their global averages; the paper’s deterministic method avoids such assumptions.
problem_definition_latex: Design a provably convergent single-loop decentralized algorithm for the stochastic DBO problem in which each agent $i$ uses only stochastic (mini-batch) derivative information from $\widehat F_i(\cdot,\cdot;\xi_i)$ and $\widehat f_i(\cdot,\cdot;\xi_i)$ and communicates only with neighbors via $W$, and prove a nonasymptotic convergence rate to a stationary point of $\Phi$ (e.g., a bound on $\min_{0\le k\le K-1}\mathbb{E}\|\nabla\Phi(\bar x_k)\|^2$ for suitable averaged iterates $\bar x_k$), without imposing any gradient/data heterogeneity assumption across agents.
latex:
- There are $n$ agents connected by a fixed, undirected communication network represented by a symmetric, doubly stochastic weight matrix $W=(w_{ij})\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$.
- The decentralized bilevel optimization (DBO) problem is
\[\min_{x\in\mathbb{R}^p}\ \Phi(x):=F(x,y^*(x)):=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n F_i(x,y^*(x))\quad\text{s.t.}\quad y^*(x)\in\arg\min_{y\in\mathbb{R}^q} f(x,y):=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n f_i(x,y).\]
- In the deterministic setting, each agent $i$ can evaluate exact derivatives of $F_i$ and $f_i$.
- In a stochastic setting, for each agent $i$ there is a random variable (data sample) $\xi_i$ such that the local objectives are expectations
\[F_i(x,y)=\mathbb{E}_{\xi_i}\big[\widehat F_i(x,y;\xi_i)\big],\qquad f_i(x,y)=\mathbb{E}_{\xi_i}\big[\widehat f_i(x,y;\xi_i)\big],\]
and agents can only access (mini-batch) stochastic gradients/Jacobians/Hessians derived from $\widehat F_i$ and $\widehat f_i$.
- The lower-level objective $f(x,\cdot)$ is (assumed) strongly convex in $y$ so that $y^*(x)$ is well-defined and unique.
- A heterogeneity assumption refers to any condition requiring the local derivatives (e.g. $\nabla \widehat f_i$ or $\nabla^2_{22}\widehat f_i$) to be uniformly close to their global averages; the paper’s deterministic method avoids such assumptions.

Design a provably convergent single-loop decentralized algorithm for the stochastic DBO problem in which each agent $i$ uses only stochastic (mini-batch) derivative information from $\widehat F_i(\cdot,\cdot;\xi_i)$ and $\widehat f_i(\cdot,\cdot;\xi_i)$ and communicates only with neighbors via $W$, and prove a nonasymptotic convergence rate to a stationary point of $\Phi$ (e.g., a bound on $\min_{0\le k\le K-1}\mathbb{E}\|\nabla\Phi(\bar x_k)\|^2$ for suitable averaged iterates $\bar x_k$), without imposing any gradient/data heterogeneity assumption across agents.
